bolster.definition: To make something stronger or better.
bolster.example: You need to bolster your goodwill when it is running low.

bids.definition: Small requests for attention or connection from someone.
bids.example: When your child says 'look at this,' that is a bid for connection.

solace.definition: Comfort during a time of sadness or difficulty.
solace.example: They found solace in each other during hard times.

adversaries.definition: People who are against you; enemies.
adversaries.example: Family members should not see each other as adversaries.

cooperation.definition: Working together towards the same goal.
cooperation.example: Cooperation makes family life much smoother.

collaboration.definition: Working together with others on a shared task.
collaboration.example: The project was a collaboration between both parents.

competition.definition: Trying to win or be better than someone else.
competition.example: Competition between partners hurts the relationship.

psychologist.definition: A person who studies how people think, feel, and behave.
psychologist.example: The psychologist studied what makes relationships work.

colleague.example: He and his colleague set up the study together.

wired.definition: Connected with equipment to measure something.
wired.example: The couples were wired up to measure their stress.

measured.definition: Found out the amount or level of something.
measured.example: Their stress levels were measured during the conversation.

calm.definition: Peaceful and not worried or excited.
calm.example: Some couples appeared calm on the outside.

sweated.definition: Produced liquid from the skin because of heat or stress.
sweated.example: The stressed couples sweated a lot during the talk.

fight-or-flight.definition: The body's automatic reaction to danger: either fight back or run away.
fight-or-flight.example: Their bodies were in fight-or-flight mode even during a calm talk.

revelation.example: The real revelation came six years later.

follow-up.definition: A later check or meeting to see what happened since the first time.
follow-up.example: At the follow-up session, researchers checked on the couples.

dysfunctional.definition: Not working properly; having serious problems.
dysfunctional.example: The high-stress couples were in dysfunctional relationships.

disasters.definition: Very bad events or situations; here, couples whose relationships failed.
disasters.example: Gottman called the struggling couples the Disasters.

masters.definition: People who are very skilled; here, couples with strong relationships.
masters.example: The Masters had calm, healthy partnerships.

indicators.definition: Signs or signals that show something.
indicators.example: High stress indicators predicted relationship problems.

hostile.example: Feeling stressed makes you act in a hostile way.

hostility.definition: Unfriendly or aggressive feelings towards someone.
hostility.example: Hostility between partners hurts the whole family.

vicious.definition: Very bad and harmful, often getting worse in a cycle.
vicious.example: It becomes a vicious circle of anger and hurt.

one-upmanship.definition: Trying to show you are better than someone else.
one-upmanship.example: One-upmanship is common but harmful in relationships.

superior.example: Advertising makes people feel superior to others.

desirable.example: Ads try to make you feel sexually desirable.

smug.example: The ad makes you feel smug about your choice.

conversely.example: Conversely, calm couples are warmer to each other.

soothed.definition: Made to feel calm and comfortable.
soothed.example: Feeling soothed by your partner makes you warmer.

affectionate.example: Calm couples are more affectionate with each other.

socializing.definition: Spending time with other people for fun.
socializing.example: He observed couples socializing at a holiday home.

refers.definition: Calls or names something in a particular way.
refers.example: Gottman refers to these requests as bids for connection.

satisfied.definition: Happy because your needs or wants have been met.
satisfied.example: Their bid for connection was satisfied when the other listened.

reciprocal.definition: Given or done in return; going both ways.

sustained.definition: Kept going over a long period of time.
sustained.example: Without these small acts, relationships cannot be sustained.

responsive.definition: Quick to react or answer in a helpful way.
responsive.example: Be responsive when your child asks for attention.

scan.definition: To look at quickly to find something specific.
scan.example: You can scan for faults or look for things to appreciate.

criticism.definition: Saying what is wrong with someone or something.
criticism.example: Criticism hurts more than it helps.

tipping.definition: Moving the balance from one side to another.
tipping.example: Tipping the scales from criticism to appreciation changes everything.

scales.definition: A device for measuring weight; here, the balance between two things.
scales.example: The scales were tipped towards criticism in her family.

unbalanced.definition: Not equal; one side is heavier than the other.

crucial.example: Appreciation is crucial in a healthy partnership.

toxic.example: Constant criticism feels like bathing in toxic soup.

bathing.definition: Covering yourself in something, like water.
bathing.example: Old habits felt like bathing in a toxic soup of criticism.

victim.definition: A person who has been hurt or treated unfairly.
victim.example: Being kind does not mean being a victim.

unassertive.definition: Not confident enough to say what you want.
unassertive.example: Kindness does not mean being unassertive.

intentionally.example: Even actions done unintentionally can hurt someone.

validate.definition: To show that someone's feelings are real and important.
validate.example: It is important to validate how they feel.

defensive.definition: Protecting yourself from being blamed or criticized.
defensive.example: Try not to become defensive when someone tells you how they feel.

counsel.example: Some people counsel dealing with small problems early.

irritations.definition: Small things that annoy or bother you.
irritations.example: Deal with minor irritations before they become big.

empathized.definition: Understood and shared another person's feelings.
empathized.example: Everyone benefits from being empathized with.

priority.definition: The most important thing that should be done first.
priority.example: Make understanding each other a priority in your family.

maintenance.definition: The work needed to keep something in good condition.
maintenance.example: Turning towards bids is a major part of relationship maintenance.

precious.example: Relationships are precious and need care.

product.definition: Something that is made or created by a process.
product.example: We are a product of our environment.

environment.definition: The conditions and people around you that affect your life.
environment.example: A healthy environment helps children grow.

unilaterally.definition: Done by one side or person alone, without others.
unilaterally.example: If you are unilaterally kind, your partner will notice.

catching.definition: Spreading easily from one person to another.
catching.example: Kindness is catching — others will copy it.
